sections.title: Overview
sections.content: This guideline outlines how physiotherapists should approach work-capacity statements, recommendations, and restrictions in FIFOEMS settings.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Worker asking for clearance, restrictions, or progression
- Supervisor requesting work-capacity advice
- Reassessment after injury or illness

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Fitness-for-work advice should be based on function, risk, and task demands, not a simple statement of pain
- The physio role is to support safe participation and clear restrictions, while staying within legal and organisational certification rules
- Safety-critical tasks need a higher threshold for confidence than low-risk desk or admin duties

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Clarify essential tasks, current symptoms, and immediate safety risks
- If the worker appears unsafe, medically unstable, or clearly outside onsite management, escalate rather than issue broad reassurance
- Avoid vague terms such as 'light duties' without defining what they mean

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Give specific limits around lifting, climbing, kneeling, driving, repetitive force, walking distance, and shift duration where applicable
- State review points and what improvement is needed before progression
- Coordinate with the doctor or employer when local policy requires formal certification outside physio authority

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Useful work-capacity advice tells the workplace what is safe today and what needs to change before more is safe tomorrow
